content.assessment: This baseline proposal relies on demographic contraction rather than any comprehensive-schooling design principle, so it is largely outside the explanatory scope of the Finnish lens, which concerns intentional structural design (tracking age, comprehensive provision) rather than demographically forced capacity adjustment. The lens evidence does not directly address demographic-driven consolidation, but the Finnish case is nonetheless informative by contrast: Finland's equity gains stemmed from deliberate structural design (untracked, comprehensive, age-16 first selection) rather than passive capacity shrinkage, and even that deliberate design has proven vulnerable to erosion via informal sorting and choice effects over time (F-live-finnish_reform-07, F-live-finnish_reform-08, F-live-finnish_reform-09). By implication, a purely passive, non-structural approach that does not even attempt deliberate comprehensive design is a weaker candidate for durable equity improvement than Finland's active (if imperfectly sustained) reform, since it leaves the tracking age, admissions mechanism, and sorting channels entirely unaddressed and subject to geographically uneven, potentially inequality-worsening effects (as the proposal's own risk notes about urban flagship persistence).
opportunities: None directly derivable from the Finnish lens, since this proposal does not engage comprehensive-schooling design; any equity benefit would be incidental to demographic trends rather than attributable to reform design
strengths: Provides a genuine counterfactual baseline against which more active reforms (t1-t4) can be compared for incremental effect, which is methodologically useful regardless of substantive alignment with the Finnish model
threats: Even Finland's actively designed and initially successful comprehensive reform has experienced rising SES achievement gaps and informal sorting over recent decades (F-live-finnish_reform-07, F-live-finnish_reform-08); a purely passive approach with no active design principle offers no comparable mechanism to counteract analogous or worse sorting dynamics in the Hungarian context
weaknesses: Contains no analog to Finland's core design features (delayed, comprehensive, untracked lower secondary); passive contraction does not address the age-10/12 tracking point identified as the key inequality-associated variable (F-live-finnish_reform-05, F-live-finnish_reform-06)

Uneven geographic contraction (urban flagship persistence vs. rural decline) risks a pattern analogous to, but potentially worse than, Finnish school-choice-driven segregation (F-live-finnish_reform-09), since it operates with no counteracting comprehensive design principle at all
